sections.title: EW Industry Brief
paragraphs: The Association of Old Crows (AOC) released its quarterly threat outlook this week, with continued emphasis on adaptive jamming techniques observed across contested regions in Eastern Europe and the Indo-Pacific. Cognitive EW — systems that learn and counter signal environments in real time — remains the dominant industry investment area, with multiple primes announcing new SDR-based platforms targeting fielded delivery within 24 months.

On the policy side, the FY26 NDAA markup includes expanded funding for EW range modernization and a new line item for spectrum-sharing trials between DoD and commercial 5G operators. Watch for committee hearings later this month — the appropriations sub-committee is pushing back on the 5G coexistence language, citing concerns about active jamming exercises in proximity to commercial bands.

On the contracts front: a new Tactical EW System (TEWS) IDIQ awarded last Friday with a $1.4B ceiling, three primes selected. First task orders expected within 60 days. Industry analysts read this as a sign the Army is committing to a multi-vendor approach rather than concentrating on a single integrator — a meaningful shift from the previous decade's pattern.

Counter-UAS continues to dominate the requirements pipeline. The Joint Counter-Small Unmanned Aircraft Systems Office (JCO) released a refreshed list of approved systems this week, with three new RF-based effectors added — all featuring software-defined waveforms designed to counter the FPV-drone threat that has dominated lessons learned out of Ukraine. Expect a wave of supplemental budget asks tied to forward-deployed rotational units.

Workforce signals: the Naval Postgraduate School announced an expanded EW curriculum with two new graduate certificates focused on machine-learning-driven signal classification, opening fall 2026. Industry hiring trends mirror this — LinkedIn data shows a 38% YoY increase in postings explicitly tagging 'AI/ML for EW' as a required skill, concentrated in the Huntsville, Crane, and Patuxent River clusters.

International watch: AUKUS Pillar 2 working groups quietly stood up a new EW thread last month, focused on shared signal libraries and cross-platform threat data. Classification reciprocity has historically been the choke point on this kind of cooperation.

sections.title: Cyber & Pentesting Roundup
paragraphs: A critical authentication bypass (CVE-2026-XXXX) was disclosed in a widely-deployed VPN appliance, with active exploitation observed before the patch dropped. CISA added it to the KEV catalog within 48 hours. If you maintain edge inventory: triage now. Multiple incident responders are reporting initial-access broker chatter referencing the vuln within hours of public disclosure — patch latency is the entire game here.

Defcon CFP closed Sunday — accepted talks notify in early June. Early signal from the review committees suggests heavy emphasis on supply-chain attacks (post-XZ era) and AI-assisted exploitation tooling. The traditional 'mainstage' vs 'village' split is becoming more permeable; expect crossover programming.

On the offensive tooling front, a new open-source post-exploitation framework gained traction this week for its low-EDR-footprint approach to lateral movement in hybrid AD environments. Notable design choice: zero PowerShell, zero WMI calls — entirely COM-based, which evades most modern behavioral detections out of the box. Defenders should be tuning ETW correlations now.

Ransomware roundup: three new affiliate programs surfaced this week, two of which are explicitly recruiting Initial Access Brokers with prior healthcare or municipal access. The 'big game hunting' shift continues — fewer total intrusions, larger average ransom demand, and more frequent double-extortion. The HHS sector alert this week emphasized incident-response retainer hygiene over new technical controls; if you're at a hospital system, that's the lever to pull.

Cloud security: a misconfigured AWS S3 bucket exposed ~22 million records from a third-party HR analytics vendor, including SSN and salary data for several Fortune 500 customers. The bucket was discoverable for 11 weeks before disclosure — well within the median exposure window. SCP-based bucket-policy guardrails would have prevented it.

AI-assisted offensive tooling continues to mature. A research team published a working PoC showing an LLM-driven agent that performs end-to-end web app reconnaissance, finds an OWASP Top 10 vulnerability, and writes a working exploit — unattended, in under 30 minutes. Vulnerability-discovery cost is asymptoting toward zero.

sections.title: Home Network Security Report
sections.summary: Weekly automated scan of the home network behind 10.0.0.1. Eight devices enumerated. One medium-severity finding (router admin reachable from LAN with default-style cred patterns); three informational. No critical exposures.
key_findings: Router (10.0.0.1) has Telnet (port 23) exposed on LAN — legacy service, no business need.
Shinobi NVR (10.0.0.22) using default 'admin' credentials — credential stuffing risk if firewall is misconfigured.
Hisense TV (10.0.0.45) running ports 7000 and 8080 with unknown services — likely vendor remote-control protocols, advertising on mDNS.
recommendations: Disable Telnet on router admin: log in to 10.0.0.1 → Advanced → Remote Management → disable 'Telnet to router'.
Change Shinobi NVR admin password to a 20+ character random string. Disable LAN-only superuser if possible.
Isolate the Hisense TV on a guest VLAN — it does not need access to the rest of the home network. Most NETGEAR firmware supports a 'Guest WiFi' that's segregated.
Enable WPA3 if not already on (settings → Wireless → Security).

sections.title: CrossFit News
paragraphs: Quarterfinals wrapped this past weekend with several notable upsets in the Individual category — multiple returning Games athletes finished outside the cutoff, opening the field for a younger 2026 class. Top of the leaderboard saw a returning podium athlete in commanding form heading into Semifinals.

On the affiliate side, CrossFit HQ announced refreshed Open programming guidance focused on movement standard consistency across regions, following the judging controversies that surfaced during the 25.3 workout. Expect a more conservative approach to no-rep tolerances in 26.1.

Athlete watch: Justin Medeiros confirmed in a podcast appearance that the back injury that cost him 2025 is fully cleared and he's training at full intensity again. His return to competition is one of the season's biggest narratives.

Semifinals format update: HQ confirmed the regional split returns to four NA Semifinal events for 2026, scrapping the consolidated two-event experiment from last season. Athletes get more rest days between weekends, and travel costs come down for the cutoff bubble — a win-win after the 2025 logistics complaints. Workout reveals begin three weeks before each event, same as last year.

Programming trends: top affiliate programs continue to adopt percentage-based strength cycles paired with metcon volume that's actually been dialed back vs. 2024 numbers. The shift away from 'more is more' is showing up in coaching certifications too — the Level 2 curriculum was updated this spring to emphasize recovery markers (HRV, sleep, RPE) over raw output. If your box hasn't started talking about deloads as a programmed feature instead of an emergency fix, that's a signal worth raising.

Masters & Teens: the new Age Group Online Qualifier registration numbers dropped this week — Masters 35-39 saw a 28% YoY bump, the largest growth bracket. Teens 14-15 grew 19%. The sport's pipeline health is mostly the story of the bottom and the top of the age curve.

sections.title: College Football Watch
paragraphs: Spring transfer portal closed with several quarterback movements that will reshape preseason rankings — most notably a Power 4 starter heading to a SEC West contender, immediately altering that program's projected ceiling. Expect updated SP+ ratings to drop midweek.

On the schedule front, two ranked nonconference matchups were finalized for Week 1, including a primetime kickoff that's already drawing playoff implications discussion. ABC and Fox both pushing hard for the broadcast slot.

NIL note: a top-five recruiting class commit-flipped to a different conference last weekend after an NIL collective restructured its payouts. The portal economy continues to shift power dynamics — coaches are quietly admitting recruiting is now a year-round budget exercise rather than a relationship-building one.

Conference realignment ripples continue: a remaining Pac-12 holdout finalized its move to a new conference home this week, ending two years of speculation. The travel budget implications for non-revenue sports are significant — expect to see more reporting later this summer on how athletic departments are restructuring around the new Olympic-sport travel realities.

Coaching carousel — early summer edition: two SEC defensive coordinators are quietly being courted by Power 4 programs for 2027 head-coaching openings that don't technically exist yet. The 'whisper-network' hire is becoming the new model; by the time a job is officially posted, the search is already 80% done. Buyout language in current contracts is being structured accordingly.

Playoff format: the CFP management committee meets next month to vote on the rumored 14-team expansion for 2027. The 5+9 model (five conference champs, nine at-large) appears to have momentum, though the SEC and Big Ten are pushing for guaranteed multi-bid floors.

sections.title: Something Good in the World
paragraphs: A retired teacher in rural Kentucky paid off the lunch debt for every student in three local school districts — a quiet act of generosity that has since inspired a community fund collecting donations to do the same statewide. Organizers report over $80,000 raised in the first week, with multiple districts already cleared.

In a separate story: a Nashville coffee shop owner started a 'pay what you can' Tuesday after noticing several regulars stretching food budgets at the end of the month. The shop now sees its highest revenue day on Tuesdays — locals tipping forward to cover others. Worth knowing about a city.

From Memphis: an 11-year-old started a sidewalk lemonade stand to raise money for his elementary school's art program after the district cut funding. He's raised over $14,000 in six weeks. Local hardware-store owners donated lumber for an upgraded stand; a regional bank matched the running total. The art program is now funded for the next two school years.

Internationally: a Polish village hosting Ukrainian refugee families since 2022 just celebrated its first 'mixed' wedding — a Polish baker and a Ukrainian schoolteacher who met when she came in for bread the week she arrived. The mayor officiated. The village paper ran a one-line headline that translates roughly to 'Three years later, the bread did its work.'

A research note worth carrying: a longitudinal study followed 2,400 adults over 15 years and found that the single strongest predictor of life satisfaction at age 60 wasn't income, marital status, or career success — it was the number of weak-tie relationships maintained. Casual hellos compound.
Small ripple. Big wave. Worth remembering on a Monday.

sections.title: Verse to Memorize
sections.verse_text: Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the Lord your God will be with you wherever you go.
sections.reflection:
A verse worth carrying through the week. Joshua received this charge as he was about to lead a people into unknown territory after Moses had died — the strength is not self-generated, it's anchored in presence. When Monday brings something hard, the question isn't 'am I capable?' — it's 'who is with me?' Try memorizing it by Wednesday. Repeat it in the truck on the way to PT, in line at the commissary, before any conversation you've been dreading. Words that get repeated become words that get believed.

Context that often gets lost: the command to be strong and courageous shows up three times in the first nine verses of Joshua — verse 6, verse 7, and verse 9. The repetition isn't God being redundant; it's God knowing exactly how loud the fear in Joshua's head must have been at that moment. He had spent forty years in Moses's shadow. Now Moses is dead and the river is in front of him. The triple charge is a pattern: when the assignment scales, the reassurance scales with it.

The Hebrew word translated 'courageous' here is *amats* — to be alert, to make firm, to take a stand. It's an active word, not a feeling. Courage here isn't the absence of fear; it's the presence of resolve in spite of it. That distinction matters when Wednesday comes around and you don't *feel* strong — the verse isn't asking you to manufacture an emotion, it's asking you to take the next step.

Application: pair this verse with one concrete decision this week you've been putting off because of low-grade fear — a hard conversation, a ministry ask, a career move. Read the verse, take the step, repeat. By Friday, you'll have it memorized and a piece of evidence that the promise holds.

sections.title: Last Week on Strava
sections.summary:
Solid volume week — three runs and two strength sessions. Easy pace held steady at 8:42/mi on the long run; week-over-week elevation up 18%. (Strava integration coming in v2 — for now this section is auto-templated; replace before sending.)

Week-over-week: total mileage +14%, average HR on easy days down 4 bpm vs. last month at comparable pace — aerobic base is building. Long-run negative split this week (8:51 first half, 8:34 second half) suggests the easy-pace discipline is paying off. Sleep averaged 7h 12m; lowest night was Wednesday at 5h 48m, which lines up with the elevated resting HR Thursday morning. Worth protecting Wednesday nights heading into peak weeks.

Next week's focus: progression long run (12 mi w/ final 3 at marathon pace), one threshold session, and a CrossFit-emphasis day mid-week. Skip the second easy run if Wednesday goes hard — recovery > volume at this stage of the cycle.

sections.title: Opening of the Week
sections.intro: A solid, strategically rich opening for White starting with 1.d4 followed by 2.Nf3 and 3.Bf4. Recommended for club players who want a 'system' approach with low theory burden but real winning chances. Sometimes dismissed as 'boring,' but anyone who's faced it more than a few times knows the venom under the surface — it quietly steers the game into structures White understands better than Black.
sections.image_caption: Position after 1.d4 d5 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 — the namesake bishop on f4.
key_ideas: Develop the dark-squared bishop early to f4 — active outside the pawn chain.
Standard pawn structure: d4, e3, c3 — solid and hard to break.
Against ...Bd6 trade: meet with Bg3 or h3/Bh2 retreat.
Middlegame plan: kingside attack with Ne5, f4, sometimes Qf3-Qh3.
Watch ...c5 strikes — know when to take, push d5, or hold tension.

sections.title: Financial Life Hacks
sections.hack_name: The retention-desk call: how to cut your phone or internet bill in 15 minutes
paragraphs: If your phone or internet provider has held your bill steady (or worse, crept it up) for more than a year, you are almost certainly overpaying. The fix takes one phone call, fifteen minutes, and zero leverage beyond a single magic phrase: 'I'd like to cancel my service.' That phrase routes you out of regular customer support and into the **retention department** — a separate team whose entire job is to keep you from leaving. Retention agents have access to discounts, credits, and promotional rates that regular reps literally cannot offer.

The mechanic: providers know losing a customer costs them far more than discounting your bill. Industry CAC (customer acquisition cost) for telecom and ISP runs $300–$600 per new subscriber; cutting your monthly bill by $30–$50 to keep you is straightforward math for them. Consumer-finance reporting (Consumer Reports, NerdWallet, The Wall Street Journal) consistently finds retention departments offer 15–40% off advertised rates, plus one-time credits, faster speeds at the same price, or waived equipment fees.

Script that works: be polite, be specific, be willing to actually leave. Say: 'Hi — I've been a customer for X years, my bill is $Y, and I'm comparing to [competitor's published promo, e.g., T-Mobile $50/line, Spectrum $40/mo for 300Mbps]. I'd like to cancel today unless you can match it.' Don't argue. Don't justify. Wait. The first offer is rarely the best — if they say 'I can give you $10 off,' say 'thanks, but I need to be closer to that competitor's price to stay.' Most agents have escalation tiers; they'll keep going until they hit their cap.

Realistic numbers from public reporting and r/personalfinance threads: $20–$60 per month off a phone plan; $15–$40 per month off internet; sometimes a free upgrade (gigabit at the price of 500Mbps). Over 6 months that's $90–$360. Over 24 months — the typical promo lock — it's $360–$1,440. Worth a phone call.

Repeat every 12 months. Set a calendar reminder — promotional rates expire and providers will quietly bump you back to retail. The script works every year because the retention math doesn't change. Caveats: don't bluff if you genuinely can't leave (coverage dead zone, contracted bundle), don't accept a contract extension unless the math is clearly worth it, and always ask the agent to email a confirmation of the new rate and duration. If it's not in writing, it didn't happen.

sections.title: Meal of the Week (Serves 2)
sections.intro: Single sheet pan, one bowl for marinade, dinner on the table in 35 minutes. High protein, low effort, lots of vegetables, and the leftovers reheat well for next-day lunch. Bonus: you can swap the chicken for salmon (cut roast time to 12 min) or shrimp (8 min) without changing anything else.
sections.image_caption: Roasted herb chicken with rosemary potatoes and peppers — what plated done looks like.
ingredients: 2 boneless skinless chicken breasts (~6 oz each)
directions: Preheat oven to 425°F. Line a sheet pan with parchment.
Whisk olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, oregano, thyme, salt, pepper in a bowl.
Toss potatoes with half the marinade; spread on sheet pan. Roast 10 minutes alone.
Add chicken, broccoli, and bell pepper. Drizzle remaining marinade over everything.
Roast 18–22 min until chicken hits 165°F internal and potatoes are fork-tender. Garnish with parsley and lemon. Serve.

sections.title: Cheap Flights — RDU → BNA / PHX
sections.intro: Round-trip fares aligned with your Fort Campbell training-holiday windows. Anything below 25th-percentile of typical fare bands shown. Both BNA (closest to Clarksville) and PHX (family) are tracked weekly. Memorial Day, July 4, and Labor Day windows get pulled forward; Christmas window starts surfacing in late August.
sections.note: Prices snapshot at scan time; book within 48 hours for best chance. Fares typically tick up over the weekend. Tuesday-evening booking historically returns the lowest median price for Southwest legs, per the last 90 days of internal scrape data. PHX legs price-floor in mid-week departures; BNA legs are insensitive to day-of-week. If a fare drops below $130 to BNA or below $250 to PHX, that's typically the bottom — book it.

sections.title: Tacoma Watch — White SR5 4WD < 5k mi, < $30k
sections.intro: 2020–2023 Toyota Tacoma SR5 4WD, white, under 5,000 miles, under $30,000. Tight criteria — most weeks return zero or near-zero matches. Search radius covers TN, NC, SC, VA, GA, KY, AL, FL.
sections.image_caption: Reference photo of the target trim — clean white Tacoma in TRD spec.
sections.market_note: Market context: <5k-mi SR5 4WD inventory has tightened ~22% YoY; expect <$30k listings to stay rare through Q3. Best buying window historically is late September after dealers move new inventory.
sections.empty_message: No matches this week — criteria are tight. Will keep watching.

sections.title: Morgan Wallen Watch (within 50mi of Raleigh)

concert.artist: Morgan Wallen — I'm The Problem Tour

sections.notes: Tour dates and venue capacity drive a 30-50% pricing premium on listings within 2 mi for the night before and night of. Booking 90+ days out and avoiding cancellation flexibility usually saves 15-20%. NC State graduation weekend overlaps Aug 21-23 — expect tighter inventory and surge pricing across the entire 10-mile radius. If you decide to go, the walk to Lenovo Center from anything downtown is faster than rideshare on event night.
sections.empty_message: No upcoming Morgan Wallen concerts within 50 mi of Raleigh in the next 12 months. Will keep watching.

sections.title: Alpaca Paper Trading — Weekly Snapshot
sections.summary:
Quiet week overall — broad indices flat, your tech-heavy positions outperformed mid-week on chip earnings. Notable winner: NVDA up 6.2% intraweek. Notable laggard: a small consumer-discretionary position you've held since March.

Market context: VIX closed the week at 14.2 — historically low and consistent with the post-earnings calm. 10-year Treasury yields drifted slightly lower mid-week on softer-than-expected PPI, then snapped back Friday. Tech outperformance is being driven by data-center-capex narratives, not by consumer demand — keep that distinction in mind when sizing future positions.

Defense sector watch: with the TEWS IDIQ awarded this week (see EW brief on page 3), three of the named primes are publicly traded. Worth a look if you want sector exposure aligned with your professional reading. None added to portfolio yet — running a thesis check first.
